subject: Statistics
title: What Is Bayesian Thinking?
description: A 60-second lesson on What Is Bayesian Thinking?.

hook.text: You hear hoofbeats. Is it horses or zebras? Your answer depends on whether you're in Kentucky or Kenya.

buildup.text: Bayesian thinking starts with what you already know — your prior — then updates it with new evidence.

discovery.text: Thomas Bayes discovered the formula in the 1700s. It tells you exactly how much to shift your beliefs.

twist.text: Most people ignore base rates. A positive test doesn't mean you're sick if the disease is extremely rare.

climax.text: Bayesian thinking is a framework for rational belief. It tells you how to think, not what to think.

punchline.text: Start with what you know. Update with what you learn.

quiz.question: What makes Bayesian thinking different from other approaches?
options: It starts with prior beliefs and systematically updates them with new evidence
It only uses data and ignores prior knowledge
It requires exact knowledge of the truth before testing
